lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Nancy Drew? Oh, thank heavens. I wasn't sure anyone would come.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Ms. Delacroix? You called about a poisoning — and a ghost?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Both, dear. Roland Ashworth — the man demolishing my theater — collapsed last night. Doctors say he was poisoned. And before that... she appeared again.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine. The greatest actress who ever stood on that stage. She died here in 1928 — on stage, during her final performance. And now her ghost walks the Monarch.

choices.text: Tell me about Margaux Fontaine.
What happened to Ashworth?
Who else is in the theater right now?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine was a legend. She was performing 'The Crimson Veil' — her signature role — when she collapsed during the final act. Poison in the prop goblet, they said. An accident.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: But I never believed that. She was my godmother, Nancy. She held me as a baby. And I've spent my whole life in this theater, waiting for the truth.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You think she was murdered?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I know she was. And I think her ghost knows it too.

choices.text: What happened to Ashworth?
I'll start investigating.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Roland Ashworth bought the Monarch six months ago. He plans to demolish it in three days and build luxury condominiums.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Last night, he was found unconscious in his office. The doctors say it was some kind of toxin — non-lethal, but deliberate. He's recovering upstairs, furious and suspicious of everyone.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Does he have enemies here?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Everyone in this theater has a reason to want the demolition stopped. Myself included.

choices.text: Tell me about Margaux Fontaine.
I'll start looking around.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: There's Edwin Hale — a theater historian. He's been documenting the Monarch for years. You'll find him in the auditorium, practically living in those seats.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Stella Morrow is our stage manager. Practical woman, knows every inch of this building. She's backstage.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: And young Diego Reyes — a playwright renting the projection booth as a writing studio. Sweet boy, but he's heard things at night he can't explain.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Then there's Ashworth himself, recovering in the manager's office. He won't be happy to see a detective.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I left a master key at the concierge desk. It opens most doors in the theater.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: And Nancy — be careful. The Monarch has kept its secrets for almost a century. It won't give them up easily.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Ah — you must be the detective Vivian called. Edwin Hale. Theater historian. Please, sit anywhere — the whole auditorium's empty, as usual.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Nancy Drew. How long have you been studying the Monarch?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Fifteen years. I've written three books on New England theater history. The Monarch is my life's work.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: This building isn't just bricks and velvet, Ms. Drew. It's a living archive. Every floorboard has a story. And if Ashworth has his way, it'll all be rubble in seventy-two hours.

choices.text: Tell me about The Crimson Veil.
What do you think about the ghost sightings?
Did you see anything suspicious last night?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: The Crimson Veil was the jewel of the 1928 season. A gothic melodrama about a countess who discovers her husband has been poisoning her. Ironic, isn't it?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine was extraordinary in the role. In the final act, her character drinks from a poisoned goblet. But on opening night — October 31st — the prop goblet contained real poison.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And it was ruled an accident?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: The official story was that a stagehand made a terrible mistake. But the stagehand was never identified. And Margaux's understudy, Cecilia Drake, took over the role the very next night and became a star.

choices.text: You think Cecilia Drake killed her?
Thank you, Edwin. I'll keep investigating.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I'm a historian, not a spiritualist. But I've seen things here I can't explain.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Fog that rolls across the stage when no machine is running. A figure in white that appears under the ghost light and vanishes. The sound of a woman's voice reciting lines from The Crimson Veil.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Whether it's truly Margaux's spirit or something else entirely... this theater is not at peace.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I was in my usual seat — row G, center — reviewing my notes. Around ten o'clock, I heard a crash from the direction of the manager's office. By the time I got there, Ashworth was on the floor.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Stella called the ambulance. The rest of us waited. It was... unsettling.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Did you notice anything else? Anyone missing?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Everyone was accounted for. But this is a big building with many hidden corners. Someone could have slipped away without being seen.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I have my suspicions. Cecilia Drake had means, motive, and opportunity. She knew the prop table. She had access backstage. And she had everything to gain.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: But proving it after nearly a century? That's the challenge. If there's evidence, it's buried somewhere in this theater.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Then I'd better start digging.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: If you need anything — history, floor plans, old records — I'm your man. I know this building better than anyone alive.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Oh, and Nancy? If you see the ghost... try talking to her. You never know.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Detective? Great. Another person poking around my backstage. I'm Stella Morrow — stage manager. Or I was, until Ashworth decided this place was worth more as rubble.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You don't seem happy about the investigation.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I'm not happy about any of this. A man was poisoned, there's supposedly a ghost, and in three days this whole building comes down. I just want to do my job and go home.

choices.text: Has anything gone missing from backstage?
Do you know how the special effects work?
Where were you when Ashworth collapsed?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Some things have... been relocated. For safekeeping. Valuable antique props, costumes — they shouldn't be destroyed with the building.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Relocated where, exactly?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Look, those props are irreplaceable. I did what I had to do. That doesn't make me a poisoner.

choices.text: I'm not accusing you. Tell me about the effects system.
We'll come back to the props later.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: The Monarch was state-of-the-art in 1925. Fog machines, trapdoors, a fly system, even hidden passages behind the walls for quick costume changes.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: There should be an effects manual on the tech shelf — a big binder. It has diagrams of everything.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Could someone use those systems to fake a ghost?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: If they knew what they were doing? Absolutely. Fog, a trapdoor entrance, the right lighting cue... you could make someone appear and vanish in seconds.

choices.text: Who would know the systems well enough?
Thanks, Stella. I'll check out that manual.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Me, obviously. Edwin Hale probably — he's studied every inch of this place. Vivian grew up here, so she'd know the basics.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: But the hidden passages? Those aren't common knowledge. You'd need the original blueprints or the effects manual to even know they exist.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I was right here, backstage, doing inventory. When I heard the crash I ran to the office and called 911. You can check the call log.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I didn't poison anyone. I don't even like tea.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: One more thing — there's a locked door at the back of backstage that leads to the basement. I don't have the key. Nobody's supposed to. But I've heard the door opening and closing at night.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Whatever's going on in this theater... it's coming from down there.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Oh! Sorry — didn't hear you come up. I get lost in my writing. Diego Reyes. I'm renting the projection booth as a studio.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Nancy Drew. I'm investigating the poisoning — and the ghost.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: The ghost is real. I mean — I've heard her. At night, from up here, I can hear someone reciting lines on the empty stage. Act three of The Crimson Veil. Word for word.

choices.text: Have you found anything unusual up here?
What have you heard at night?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Actually, yes. I found an original script of The Crimson Veil in one of the film canisters. Handwritten annotations all through the margins in red ink.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I thought it was blocking notes at first, but the marks are weird. Circled letters that don't match any stage directions I know. It's like a code.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: May I take a look at it?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Be my guest. It's right here on my desk. I've been trying to figure it out for weeks.

choices.text: Thanks, Diego. This could be important.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: It started about a month ago. I stay late — the booth is quiet, good for writing. Around midnight, I hear footsteps below. Then fog drifts across the stage.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Last week, I heard what sounded like machinery — grinding, mechanical sounds from under the stage. Like old gears turning.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: And then one night... I looked down from the booth window and saw a woman in white, standing center stage. She turned, looked up — right at me — and vanished. Through the floor.

choices.text: Through the floor? Like a trapdoor?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: That's... actually a really good point. I didn't think of it as a trapdoor because — well, ghosts don't need trapdoors.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: But a person would.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Hey, if you figure out what those coded annotations mean, let me know? I'm a writer — a good mystery keeps me up at night.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: In a good way. Usually.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Another vulture. What do you want?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Nancy Drew. I'm here to find out who poisoned you, Mr. Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Oh, take your pick. Everyone in this building wants me dead. The old actress, the historian, even my own stage manager. This theater is full of people who'd rather see me in the ground than see one brick moved.

choices.text: What happened the night you collapsed?
Someone marked up your blueprints. Was that you?
You mentioned seeing someone in the building at night.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I was working late. Had a cup of tea from the kettle in here — same as every night. Within twenty minutes, my vision blurred and I couldn't stand.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Before I blacked out, I saw... someone. A shape in the doorway. They were watching me fall.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Can you describe them?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Dark clothes. Average build. They didn't help. They just watched, and then they were gone.

choices.text: Can I see the blueprints on your wall?
I'll examine the teacup.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I didn't draw those red marks. They were already there when I pinned them up. Someone's been mapping the building's hidden features — passages, sealed rooms, some kind of tunnel network.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: This theater is a maze. There are spaces behind the walls, under the stage... someone could move through this building without ever being seen.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Do you know who made the annotations?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: My money's on the historian. Hale. He's obsessed with this place. Fifteen years of research — he probably knows it better than the original architects.

choices.text: Tell me about the intruder you saw.
Thank you, Mr. Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Two weeks ago, I came in early and heard noises from below the stage. Mechanical sounds — gears, pulleys. I found the basement door unlocked and went down.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Someone had set up a workshop down there. Tools, documents, chemicals. I couldn't see everything — they heard me coming and the lights went out. I barely made it back upstairs.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: That's when the 'ghost' sightings started getting worse. And three days later, I was poisoned.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You think whoever's in the basement poisoned you to keep you quiet?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I don't think. I know.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Find whoever did this. And find them fast. Because in seventy-two hours, this building comes down regardless.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Ghost or no ghost.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Hello, Edwin.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Nancy. I was wondering when you'd find your way down here.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The fog machines. The trapdoor. The white costume. Your notebook on the catwalk. You're the ghost of the Monarch Theatre.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: And you're as good as Vivian said you were.

choices.text: Why, Edwin? Why all of this?
You poisoned Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Fifteen years I've been researching this theater. And six months ago, I found them — Cecilia Drake's letters to a pharmacist. She ordered the exact compound that killed Margaux.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine was murdered by her own understudy. And nobody ever knew. Cecilia stole her role, built a career on it, and died celebrated in 1987.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I had the proof. But Ashworth had the demolition permits. In three days, this evidence would be buried under concrete. So I did the only thing I could think of.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I made Margaux's ghost real enough to matter. If the Monarch was haunted — truly haunted — the historical society could petition for landmark status. The demolition would be delayed. The truth would come out.

choices.text: But then Ashworth caught you.
That's why you poisoned him — to buy time.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Ashworth found the basement. He saw my setup. He was going to call the police and accelerate the demolition.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I used a compound from Margaux's alchemy book — the one in the dressing room. Non-lethal. Enough to make him too ill to sign demolition orders for a few days.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You poisoned a man, Edwin.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I saved a theater. I saved a woman's legacy. I saved the truth about a murder that went unsolved for a century.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Was it wrong? Yes. Would I do it again? ...Yes.

choices.text: I'm calling the police. You poisoned someone, Edwin.
Let's go public. The 1928 murder, the cover-up — all of it. Together.
Walk away, Edwin. Disappear. I'll make sure Margaux's story gets told.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I understand. I knew this was how it might end.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Just... promise me one thing. Make sure the letters get to the right people. Margaux deserves to be remembered for more than how she died.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I promise.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Together? You'd do that?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The world should know what happened to Margaux Fontaine. And they should know what you did to protect her memory — all of it. The heroism and the crime.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Then let's give the Monarch one last opening night.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You'd let me go?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm letting Margaux's story be told. What happens to you... that's between you and your conscience.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Thank you, Nancy. Tell Vivian I'm sorry I couldn't say goodbye.
